Hello, world

); ``` -------------------------------- ### Render a React component Source: https://github.com/reactjs/react.dev/blob/main/src/content/learn/add-react-to-an-existing-project.md Example demonstrating how to clear existing HTML content and render a basic 'Hello, world' React component using createRoot. ```html My app

); ``` -------------------------------- ### Rendering a React component into an existing HTML element Source: https://github.com/reactjs/react.dev/blob/main/src/content/learn/add-react-to-an-existing-project.md Demonstrates how to integrate a React component into a specific HTML element on a page, preserving existing HTML content. ```html My app

{"type":"message_update","assistantMessageEvent":{"type":"text_delta","contentIndex":1,"delta":"# React Server Components (RSC)

## What they are

React Server Components (RSC)

## What they are

React Server Components (RSC)

## What they are

**React Server Components (RSC)** are components that run **only on the server** and render into the React tree before HTML is sent to the client.  
They are mainly used in frameworks like **Next.js (App Router)** and let you keep data fetching + rendering logic off the browser, while sending only what the user can see/need.

They do **not** ship their code to the React Server Components (RSC)

## What they are

**React Server Components (RSC)** are components that run **only on the server** and render into the React tree before HTML is sent to the client.  
They are mainly used in frameworks like **Next.js (App Router)** and let you keep data fetching + rendering logic off the browser, while sending only what the user can see/need.

They do **not** ship their code to the browser,"}],"api":"openai-codex-responses","provider":"openai-codex","model":"gpt-5.3-codex-spark","usage":{"input":0,"output":0,"cacheRead":0,"cacheWrite":0,"totalTokens":0,"cost":{"input":0,"output":0,"cacheRead":0,"cacheWrite":0,"total":0}},"stopReason":"stop","timestamp":1781134155497,"responseId":"resp_0ae6d25a9e2ebbfc016a29f34c350481918c7bd9bb0d609d47"}}

{"type":"message_update","assistantMessageEvent":{"type":"text_delta","contentIndex":1,"delta":" unlike normal React components that run client-side.

## Why use them

- Move data fetching and heavy rendering work to the server.
- Reduce client JavaScript bundle size.

React Server Components (RSC)

## What they are

**React Server Components (RSC)** are components that run **only on the server** and render into the React tree before HTML is sent to the client.  
They are mainly used in frameworks like **Next.js (App Router)** and let you keep data fetching + rendering logic off the browser, while sending only what the user can see/need.

They do **not** ship their code to the browser, unlike normal React components that run client-side.

## Why use them

- Move data fetching and heavy rendering work to the server.
- Reduce client JavaScript bundle size.
- Let frameworks stream UI in chunks for faster first paint.

React Server Components (RSC)

## What they are

**React Server Components (RSC)** are components that run **only on the server** and render into the React tree before HTML is sent to the client.  
They are mainly used in frameworks like **Next.js (App Router)** and let you keep data fetching + rendering logic off the browser, while sending only what the user can see/need.

They do **not** ship their code to the browser, unlike normal React components that run client-side.

## Why use them

- Move data fetching and heavy rendering work to the server.
- Reduce client JavaScript bundle size.
- Let frameworks stream UI in chunks for faster first paint.

## Server vs Client Components

| Concern | Server Component | Client Component |
|---|---|---|

React Server Components (RSC)

## What they are

**React Server Components (RSC)** are components that run **only on the server** and render into the React tree before HTML is sent to the client.  
They are mainly used in frameworks like **Next.js (App Router)** and let you keep data fetching + rendering logic off the browser, while sending only what the user can see/need.

They do **not** ship their code to the browser, unlike normal React components that run client-side.

## Why use them

- Move data fetching and heavy rendering work to the server.
- Reduce client JavaScript bundle size.
- Let frameworks stream UI in chunks for faster first paint.
- Still combine with interactive client parts where needed.

## Server vs Client Components

| Concern | Server Component | Client Component |
|---|---|---|

React Server Components (RSC)

## What they are

**React Server Components (RSC)** are components that run **only on the server** and render into the React tree before HTML is sent to the client.  
They are mainly used in frameworks like **Next.js (App Router)** and let you keep data fetching + rendering logic off the browser, while sending only what the user can see/need.

They do **not** ship their code to the browser, unlike normal React components that run client-side.

## Why use them

- Move data fetching and heavy rendering work to the server.
- Reduce client JavaScript bundle size.
- Let frameworks stream UI in chunks for faster first paint.
- Still combine with interactive client parts where needed.

## Server vs Client Components

| Concern | Server Component | Client Component |
|---|---|---|
| Runs in | Server (Node/edge runtime) | Browser |

React Server Components (RSC)

## What they are

**React Server Components (RSC)** are components that run **only on the server** and render into the React tree before HTML is sent to the client.  
They are mainly used in frameworks like **Next.js (App Router)** and let you keep data fetching + rendering logic off the browser, while sending only what the user can see/need.

They do **not** ship their code to the browser, unlike normal React components that run client-side.

## Why use them

- Move data fetching and heavy rendering work to the server.
- Reduce client JavaScript bundle size.
- Let frameworks stream UI in chunks for faster first paint.
- Still combine with interactive client parts where needed.

## Server vs Client Components

| Concern | Server Component | Client Component |
|---|---|---|
| Runs in | Server (Node/edge runtime) | Browser |
| Can use Hooks (`useState`, `useEffect`) | No | Yes |

React Server Components (RSC)

## What they are

**React Server Components (RSC)** are components that run **only on the server** and render into the React tree before HTML is sent to the client.  
They are mainly used in frameworks like **Next.js (App Router)** and let you keep data fetching + rendering logic off the browser, while sending only what the user can see/need.

They do **not** ship their code to the browser, unlike normal React components that run client-side.

## Why use them

- Move data fetching and heavy rendering work to the server.
- Reduce client JavaScript bundle size.
- Let frameworks stream UI in chunks for faster first paint.
- Still combine with interactive client parts where needed.

## Server vs Client Components

| Concern | Server Component | Client Component |
|---|---|---|
| Runs in | Server (Node/edge runtime) | Browser |
| Can use Hooks (`useState`, `useEffect`) | No | Yes |
| Can access DB / secrets | Yes (indirectly via server APIs) | No |
| Can handle user events (`onClick`, form handlers) | No | Yes"}],"api":"openai-codex-responses","provider":"openai-codex","model":"gpt-5.3-codex-spark","usage":{"input":0,"output":0,"cacheRead":0,"cacheWrite":0,"totalTokens":0,"cost":{"input":0,"output":0,"cacheRead":0,"cacheWrite":0,"total":0}},"stopReason":"stop","timestamp":1781134155497,"responseId":"resp_0ae6d25a9e2ebbfc016a29f34c350481918c7bd9bb0d609d47"}}

React Server Components (RSC)

## What they are

**React Server Components (RSC)** are components that run **only on the server** and render into the React tree before HTML is sent to the client.  
They are mainly used in frameworks like **Next.js (App Router)** and let you keep data fetching + rendering logic off the browser, while sending only what the user can see/need.

They do **not** ship their code to the browser, unlike normal React components that run client-side.

## Why use them

- Move data fetching and heavy rendering work to the server.
- Reduce client JavaScript bundle size.
- Let frameworks stream UI in chunks for faster first paint.
- Still combine with interactive client parts where needed.

## Server vs Client Components

| Concern | Server Component | Client Component |
|---|---|---|
| Runs in | Server (Node/edge runtime) | Browser |
| Can use Hooks (`useState`, `useEffect`) | No | Yes |
| Can access DB / secrets | Yes (indirectly via server APIs) | No |
| Can handle user events (`onClick`, form handlers) | No | Yes |
| Can use browser APIs | No | Yes |
| Can fetch data directly | Yes (`await` + fetch) | Usually via API calls |
| Included in JS bundle sent to client | No (default) | Yes |

React Server Components (RSC)

## What they are

**React Server Components (RSC)** are components that run **only on the server** and render into the React tree before HTML is sent to the client.  
They are mainly used in frameworks like **Next.js (App Router)** and let you keep data fetching + rendering logic off the browser, while sending only what the user can see/need.

They do **not** ship their code to the browser, unlike normal React components that run client-side.

## Why use them

- Move data fetching and heavy rendering work to the server.
- Reduce client JavaScript bundle size.
- Let frameworks stream UI in chunks for faster first paint.
- Still combine with interactive client parts where needed.

## Server vs Client Components

| Concern | Server Component | Client Component |
|---|---|---|
| Runs in | Server (Node/edge runtime) | Browser |
| Can use Hooks (`useState`, `useEffect`) | No | Yes |
| Can access DB / secrets | Yes (indirectly via server APIs) | No |
| Can handle user events (`onClick`, form handlers) | No | Yes |
| Can use browser APIs | No | Yes |
| Can fetch data directly | Yes (`await` + fetch) | Usually via API calls |
| Included in JS bundle sent to client | No (default) | Yes |

// app/page.tsx (Server Component by default)

React Server Components (RSC)

## What they are

**React Server Components (RSC)** are components that run **only on the server** and render into the React tree before HTML is sent to the client.  
They are mainly used in frameworks like **Next.js (App Router)** and let you keep data fetching + rendering logic off the browser, while sending only what the user can see/need.

They do **not** ship their code to the browser, unlike normal React components that run client-side.

## Why use them

- Move data fetching and heavy rendering work to the server.
- Reduce client JavaScript bundle size.
- Let frameworks stream UI in chunks for faster first paint.
- Still combine with interactive client parts where needed.

## Server vs Client Components

| Concern | Server Component | Client Component |
|---|---|---|
| Runs in | Server (Node/edge runtime) | Browser |
| Can use Hooks (`useState`, `useEffect`) | No | Yes |
| Can access DB / secrets | Yes (indirectly via server APIs) | No |
| Can handle user events (`onClick`, form handlers) | No | Yes |
| Can use browser APIs | No | Yes |
| Can fetch data directly | Yes (`await` + fetch) | Usually via API calls |
| Included in JS bundle sent to client | No (default) | Yes |

## Example (Next.js App Router)

```tsx
// app/page.tsx (Server Component by default)

React Server Components (RSC)

## What they are

**React Server Components (RSC)** are components that run **only on the server** and render into the React tree before HTML is sent to the client.  
They are mainly used in frameworks like **Next.js (App Router)** and let you keep data fetching + rendering logic off the browser, while sending only what the user can see/need.

They do **not** ship their code to the browser, unlike normal React components that run client-side.

## Why use them

- Move data fetching and heavy rendering work to the server.
- Reduce client JavaScript bundle size.
- Let frameworks stream UI in chunks for faster first paint.
- Still combine with interactive client parts where needed.

## Server vs Client Components

| Concern | Server Component | Client Component |
|---|---|---|
| Runs in | Server (Node/edge runtime) | Browser |
| Can use Hooks (`useState`, `useEffect`) | No | Yes |
| Can access DB / secrets | Yes (indirectly via server APIs) | No |
| Can handle user events (`onClick`, form handlers) | No | Yes |
| Can use browser APIs | No | Yes |
| Can fetch data directly | Yes (`await` + fetch) | Usually via API calls |
| Included in JS bundle sent to client | No (default) | Yes |

## Example (Next.js App Router)

```tsx
// app/page.tsx (Server Component by default)
async function getProducts() {
  const res = await fetch('https://example.com/api/products', { cache: 'no-store' });
"}],"api":"openai-codex-responses","provider":"openai-codex","model":"gpt-5.3-codex-spark","usage":{"input":0,"output":0,"cacheRead":0,"cacheWrite":0,"totalTokens":0,"cost":{"input":0,"output":0,"cacheRead":0,"cacheWrite":0,"total":0}},"stopReason":"stop","timestamp":1781134155497,"responseId":"resp_0ae6d25a9e2ebbfc016a29f34c350481918c7bd9bb0d609d47"}}

React Server Components (RSC) ## What they are **React Server Components (RSC)** are components that run **only on the server** and render into the React tree before HTML is sent to the client. They are mainly used in frameworks like **Next.js (App Router)** and let you keep data fetching + rendering logic off the browser, while sending only what the user can see/need. They do **not** ship their code to the browser, unlike normal React components that run client-side. ## Why use them - Move data fetching and heavy rendering work to the server. - Reduce client JavaScript bundle size. - Let frameworks stream UI in chunks for faster first paint. - Still combine with interactive client parts where needed. ## Server vs Client Components | Concern | Server Component | Client Component | |---|---|---| | Runs in | Server (Node/edge runtime) | Browser | | Can use Hooks (`useState`, `useEffect`) | No | Yes | | Can access DB / secrets | Yes (indirectly via server APIs) | No | | Can handle user events (`onClick`, form handlers) | No | Yes | | Can use browser APIs | No | Yes | | Can fetch data directly | Yes (`await` + fetch) | Usually via API calls | | Included in JS bundle sent to client | No (default) | Yes | ## Example (Next.js App Router) ```tsx // app/page.tsx (Server Component by default) async function getProducts() { const res = await fetch('https://example.com/api/products', { cache: 'no-store' }); return res.json() as Promise<{ id: string; name: string }[]>; } import AddToCartButton from './AddToCartButton'; export default async function Page() { const products = await getProducts(); return (

Products

    {products.map((product) => (

React Server Components (RSC) ## What they are **React Server Components (RSC)** are components that run **only on the server** and render into the React tree before HTML is sent to the client. They are mainly used in frameworks like **Next.js (App Router)** and let you keep data fetching + rendering logic off the browser, while sending only what the user can see/need. They do **not** ship their code to the browser, unlike normal React components that run client-side. ## Why use them - Move data fetching and heavy rendering work to the server. - Reduce client JavaScript bundle size. - Let frameworks stream UI in chunks for faster first paint. - Still combine with interactive client parts where needed. ## Server vs Client Components | Concern | Server Component | Client Component | |---|---|---| | Runs in | Server (Node/edge runtime) | Browser | | Can use Hooks (`useState`, `useEffect`) | No | Yes | | Can access DB / secrets | Yes (indirectly via server APIs) | No | | Can handle user events (`onClick`, form handlers) | No | Yes | | Can use browser APIs | No | Yes | | Can fetch data directly | Yes (`await` + fetch) | Usually via API calls | | Included in JS bundle sent to client | No (default) | Yes | ## Example (Next.js App Router) ```tsx // app/page.tsx (Server Component by default) async function getProducts() { const res = await fetch('https://example.com/api/products', { cache: 'no-store' }); return res.json() as Promise<{ id: string; name: string }[]>; } import AddToCartButton from './AddToCartButton'; export default async function Page() { const products = await getProducts(); return (

Products

    {products.map((product) => (
  • {product.name}
    • ))}
